summary:
The Polish-Soviet War of 1919–1921 was fought between the newly independent Republic of Poland and Soviet Russia for control of the borderlands of the former Russian Empire following the end of World War I and the Russian Revolution. Polish forces under Józef Piłsudski captured Kiev in May 1920, seeking to establish a Polish-led federation of Eastern European states as a buffer against both Russia and Germany. Soviet forces under Marshal Mikhail Tukhachevsky counterattacked and advanced to the outskirts of Warsaw by August 1920.

The Battle of Warsaw in August 1920, sometimes called the Miracle on the Vistula, proved decisive. Polish forces, using intelligence derived from breaking Soviet radio codes, launched a counterattack that routed Tukhachevsky's overextended armies. The Polish victory had significant geopolitical consequences: it halted the westward spread of the Russian Revolution into Central Europe and established Poland's eastern border considerably further east than the Curzon Line proposed by the Western powers.

The Treaty of Riga in March 1921 ended the war, with Poland retaining substantial territories in present-day Ukraine and Belarus. The peace lasted until 1939, when Germany and the Soviet Union partitioned Poland under the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact. The war remains significant in Polish historical memory as a demonstration of national resilience and military capability, and in Soviet/Russian historiography as a formative defeat.
